metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the read-count mechanism for AC (b): e.g. 'use `unittest.mock.patch("builtins.open", wraps=open)` filtered to the marker path and assert `call_count == 1` after N mutation operations in the same process.'
findings.finding: Hot-path AC (b) — 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process' — provides no mechanism to count file reads, making the 'at most once' cardinality assertion unevaluable without additional specification.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test, sub-bullet (b)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Verified by wrapping `builtins.open` with a spy filtered to the `.ensure-applied` path; after invoking the hot path twice in the same process, assert `spy.call_count == 1`.' This anchors the cardinality assertion to a concrete mechanism.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `run_ensures()` either already exists in the codebase (name the module path) or will be delivered by WS1-3 before WS5 runs; add the import path to the plan so executors can ground the tests.
findings.finding: `run_ensures()` does not exist anywhere in the repository. The plan names it as the primary callable under test in AC1 ('`run_ensures()` twice on a converged store'), AC2 ('a ticket mutation never calls `run_ensures`'), and implicitly in AC3-AC5. A repo-wide search returns zero source hits — only cached plan-review findings that previously flagged the same absence. If this function is being introduced by WS1-3, the plan must say so explicitly; if it already exists under a different name, the plan must name the real module/function.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC2, AC3, AC4, AC5 (all reference `run_ensures`); What section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `maybe_emit_pending_hint` exists (or will exist after WS1-3) and add its fully-qualified module path to the plan so the subprocess-spy test in AC2(c) can be grounded.
findings.finding: `maybe_emit_pending_hint` does not exist anywhere in the repository. The plan treats it as an already-existing callable in AC2(c): 'the write-path nudge `maybe_emit_pending_hint` spawns NO subprocess — verified by spying `subprocess` around a direct call to it'. A repo-wide search returns zero source-file hits — only cached plan-review findings.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(c); What section ('the write-path nudge maybe_emit_pending_hint itself spawns NO subprocess')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the owning module that reads/writes `.ensure-applied` (the module delivered by WS1-3) and confirm the file path convention so AC2b, AC3, and AC5 can be grounded against a real implementation.
findings.finding: `.ensure-applied` does not exist as a file path anywhere in the repository. The plan treats it as an established marker file whose read/write/parse behavior all five ACs test. A repo-wide search on 'ensure-applied' returns zero hits in source or test files — only cached plan-review findings.
findings.location: What section; AC1, AC2(b), AC3, AC5 — all reference `.ensure-applied`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit WS1 prerequisite and the expected module path for `run_ensures()` (e.g., `rebar._ensures.run_ensures`) so AC1 and AC2(a) have a verifiable import anchor.
findings.finding: `run_ensures()` does not exist anywhere in the repository. The plan names it as the primary callable under test in AC1 ('`run_ensures()` twice on a converged store') and AC2 ('a ticket mutation never calls `run_ensures`'), but no such function is found in `src/`. If this is a WS1 deliverable, the plan must state that dependency explicitly so an implementer knows where to expect the import.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 and AC2(a); What section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Depends-on: WS1 (run_ensures callable at <module_path>)' line in Scope or a preamble note in the AC block.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the WS2 prerequisite and the expected module path for `maybe_emit_pending_hint` (e.g., `rebar._writes.maybe_emit_pending_hint`) so AC2(c) has a concrete import target.
findings.finding: `maybe_emit_pending_hint` does not exist anywhere in the repository. AC2(c) requires spying `subprocess` 'around a direct call to it', but the function is absent from `src/`. The plan never names the module it will live in (presumably WS2).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2(c)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the module/callable that exposes the ensures registry id set (e.g., `rebar._ensures.REGISTRY`) so the concurrency AC assertion `parses as JSON == registry id set` has a concrete, importable reference.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC references 'the registry id set' as the expected value of the converged `.ensure-applied` JSON, but no ensures-registry (and no 'registry ids') exists anywhere in `src/`. This is a forward reference to a WS1 artifact with no stated module path, making the AC assertion unprovable without knowing where the registry is defined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency AC (AC3)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the module path of `maybe_emit_pending_hint` (e.g., `rebar._ensures`) so the `subprocess` spy in AC2(c) has a concrete patch target.
findings.finding: The module path / import location of `maybe_emit_pending_hint` is never specified in the plan, making the `subprocess` spy protocol in AC2(c) unresolvable. A spy must know the exact patch target (e.g., `unittest.mock.patch('rebar._ensures.subprocess')`) to be valid, but neither the module path nor the callable's location is defined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test AC, item (c); What section
findings.suggested_fix: Add e.g. 'patch `rebar._ensures.subprocess` (the module where `maybe_emit_pending_hint` lives) and assert `mock_subprocess.run.call_count == 0` after calling `maybe_emit_pending_hint(...)` directly'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC hot-path (b) with a named verification mechanism for the read-count constraint, e.g., 'patch `open` (or the file-read helper) to count calls to `.ensure-applied` and assert count == 1 after a simulated ticket mutation'.
findings.finding: The hot-path AC asserts `.ensure-applied` is read 'at most once per process (the cached pending set)' but names no verification mechanism — the proof is not end-state-observable as written, making the AC unverifiable by a test author without guessing the spy/mock approach.
findings.location: AC — Hot-path regression test, clause (b)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reads `.ensure-applied` at most once per process (the cached pending set)' with: 'patch the file-open call on `.ensure-applied`, run a ticket mutation, and assert the patch was called exactly once — verifying that the in-process cache prevents repeated reads'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interception mechanism for AC2(b)'s 'read at most once per process' assertion (e.g., patch `pathlib.Path.read_text` and assert call-count ≤ 1, or assert a named cache attribute is populated after the first call).
findings.finding: AC2(b)'s 'read at most once per process' assertion has no interception/counter protocol co-located with the requirement. The plan specifies a `subprocess` spy for AC2(c) but provides no analogous mock/patch/counter strategy for asserting the file-read count in AC2(b), leaving the mechanism undefined.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test AC, item (b)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact JSON schema of `.ensure-applied` (e.g., `{"applied": ["id1", "id2"]}` or a bare JSON array) so the concurrency and absent-marker AC assertions are falsifiable.
findings.finding: The `.ensure-applied` JSON schema is referenced but never defined: the concurrency AC asserts the file 'parses as JSON == the registry id set', but the plan never specifies the shape of this JSON (array of strings? object with a key? set serialized how?) nor what 'registry ids' look like as values. An executor cannot write a falsifiable assertion without knowing the expected schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test AC
findings.suggested_fix: Add a schema definition section to the plan (or reference the WS1 spec that defines it): e.g., '`.ensure-applied` is a JSON file containing a single top-level array of string IDs, e.g. `["ensure-git-config", "ensure-hooks"]`; the concurrency AC asserts `set(json.loads(content)) == REGISTRY_IDS`'.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a synchronization mechanism to the concurrency test (e.g., a pre-seeded *incomplete* or *absent* `.ensure-applied` plus a barrier — such as a `multiprocessing.Barrier` or a start-gate file — that holds both processes until both have opened the store before either writes) so the test guarantees genuine lock contention rather than sequential non-overlapping execution.
findings.finding: The concurrency test mechanism as specified may degenerate into a sequential, non-contending execution: if the first process acquires the lock, writes `.ensure-applied`, and releases before the second process starts its write attempt, the second process detects 'already converged' (idempotency) and skips writing entirely. The test then confirms convergence but never exercises the actual lock contention path — the core behavior it claims to prove. The plan specifies no synchronization barrier or start-gate to ensure both processes genuinely contend on the lock simultaneously.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test (AC3)
findings.suggested_fix: Seed the shared checkout with a known-absent `.ensure-applied`, launch both worker processes, pause each at a barrier after opening the store but before writing, then release both simultaneously. Assert that the final file is valid JSON and that both processes exited 0. This ensures the fcntl+mkdir lock actually serializes two concurrent writers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make no_mutate 'mutates nothing' concrete by naming two observable checks: (1) `git status --porcelain` shows empty output after the call, AND (2) the sweep entry-point (e.g. `run_ensures`) is patched and its `call_count == 0`.
findings.finding: The no_mutate AC — 'mutates nothing and never runs the sweep' — uses 'mutates nothing' as its observable outcome but names no concrete artifact to inspect, making the criterion hard to evaluate and leaving open whether 'nothing' means git state, filesystem, in-memory state, or all three.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — no_mutate test bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC as: 'no_mutate test: call the fsck surface with `no_mutate=True`; assert (a) `subprocess.check_output(["git","status","--porcelain"]).strip() == b""` (no git mutations) and (b) `run_ensures` mock `call_count == 0` (sweep never invoked).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the 'What' section's absent-marker description with the AC by replacing 'empty set' with 'empty set of applied ensures (pending=all)' to eliminate the ambiguity.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes the absent-marker test as 'absent/garbage `.ensure-applied` → empty set → converges' without qualifying 'empty set of WHAT', while the AC clarifies 'empty-set (pending=all)'. An implementer reading the What in isolation could interpret 'empty set' as 'no ensures are pending' (i.e., nothing to do), which is the exact opposite of the AC's intended meaning.
findings.location: What section (absent/garbage-marker description) vs AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'absent/garbage `.ensure-applied` (→ empty set → converges, no re-commit storm)' to 'absent/garbage `.ensure-applied` (→ empty set of applied ensures, i.e. pending=all → full sweep converges, no re-commit storm)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the Scope 'In' line to read 'the behavioral test suite for WS1-3 invariants' (or explicitly list 'including no_mutate behavioral tests for the fsck surface') to match the AC, OR add a clarifying parenthetical distinguishing byte-parity goldens (WS3-owned) from behavioral no_mutate tests (WS5-owned).
findings.finding: The Scope section declares 'In: the behavioral test suite for WS1-2 invariants' but the no_mutate AC (line 4) tests a 'library read-only fsck surface (no_mutate=True)' which the Scope/Out explicitly associates with WS3 ('fsck byte-parity/help goldens (WS3)'). This cross-section tension could leave an implementer uncertain whether the no_mutate behavioral test belongs in this work stream.
findings.location: Scope section ('In: the behavioral test suite for WS1-2 invariants') vs AC line 4 vs Scope Out ('fsck byte-parity/help goldens (WS3)')
findings.suggested_fix: Change Scope In to: 'the behavioral test suite for WS1-3 invariants (fsck byte-parity/help goldens remain WS3; this covers the no_mutate behavioral invariant separately from those goldens)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a timeout/deadline to the concurrency test (e.g., `multiprocessing` join with a timeout, or a `pytest-timeout` mark) and specify teardown that releases or removes the lock file if a child process exits non-zero, so a hang produces a clear failure rather than an indefinite CI block.
findings.finding: The concurrency test spawns two real OS processes that contend on a file lock (`_store/lock.py`). If either process hangs (e.g., lock not released due to a bug, stale lock, or unhandled exception inside the sweep), the test will block the CI runner indefinitely. No timeout, deadline, or cleanup teardown is specified for this test.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the concurrency AC, add: 'each subprocess is joined with a wall-clock timeout (e.g., 30 s); if either times out the test fails immediately and the lock file is removed in a `finally` block.' This satisfies the reliability requirement without changing the observable convergence assertion.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the target test file (e.g. `tests/test_ws5_behavioral.py`) and the proving command (e.g. `pytest tests/test_ws5_behavioral.py -v`) so done-ness is deterministically verifiable.
findings.finding: No test file path or runner command is specified for any AC, so an executor cannot confirm done-ness with a single deterministic command.
findings.location: Plan — Acceptance Criteria section (all bullets)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Proving command' line at the top of Acceptance Criteria: 'All criteria are verified by: `pytest tests/test_ws5_behavioral.py -v` (all tests green in CI).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the lock-file cleanup/timeout policy in the concurrency test AC and in the production lock module: e.g., lock has a TTL or is a fcntl advisory lock (auto-released on process death), and add a test asserting that a stale lock left by a killed process does not block a subsequent sweep.
findings.finding: The lifecycle of the lock file (`_store/lock.py`) on crash, signal, or test-process teardown is unspecified — no cleanup path is defined, risking lock-file accumulation/leakage between test runs or in production if a sweep process is killed.
findings.location: AC — Concurrency test: 'serialized by the store write lock, `_store/lock.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: If using an fcntl/flock advisory lock, state this explicitly (OS releases it on process death). If using a sentinel file, add a stale-lock detection step (e.g., check lock age or store PID and detect dead process) and cover it in the absent-marker or concurrency AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concurrency fixture default in the AC: e.g. 'the test registry contains exactly 3 known ids; assert `json.loads(marker) == {id1, id2, id3}`' so the cardinality check is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The concurrency AC requires the store to converge to 'exactly the registry ids' but never defines what the test fixture's registry contains or how many ids it holds, leaving an executor to guess the fixture shape.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the concurrency AC: 'Test fixture registers exactly N=3 synthetic ensures; post-convergence assertion: `assert json.loads(marker_path.read_text()) == {"id1","id2","id3"}` (len==3).' This pins the cardinality and makes the JSON-equality check executable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the verification mechanism for hot-path AC (a): e.g. 'patch `run_ensures` with `unittest.mock.patch` and assert `mock.call_count == 0` after the ticket mutation.'
findings.finding: Hot-path AC (a) — 'a ticket mutation never calls `run_ensures`' — states no verification mechanism, leaving the executor to invent one (mock? import hook? counter?), while AC (c) explicitly states 'spying subprocess around a direct call'; the asymmetry creates an execution gap.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Hot-path regression test, sub-bullet (a)
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC (a): 'verified by `unittest.mock.patch("module.run_ensures")` wrapping the mutation call; assert `mock.call_count == 0`.' Mirror the explicit spy pattern already used in AC (c).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE path for `.ensure-applied`: specify whether removed registry IDs are pruned on next sweep and add a test asserting that a store converges DOWN (stale id is removed) when a registry entry is retired.
findings.finding: No RETIRE/cleanup lifecycle is defined for `.ensure-applied` when registry entries are removed (registry shrinks). The plan only addresses convergence to the current registry id set in the concurrency AC, but never specifies whether stale IDs accumulate indefinitely or are pruned — and no test covers the shrinking-registry case.
findings.location: AC — Concurrency test / absent-marker test; 'What' section describing `.ensure-applied` lifecycle
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Shrinking-registry test: when an id present in `.ensure-applied` is removed from the registry, the next sweep rewrites the file to contain exactly the current registry ids (stale id is pruned).' Also document in the lifecycle description that the sweep is authoritative and always overwrites the file atomically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify or remove the 'old init-time git-config migration' contrast claim in AC2(c); if it refers to a real historical code path, name the commit or module where it lived.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the old init-time git-config migration' as a known contrast point for AC2(c)'s no-subprocess claim ('unlike the old init-time git-config migration'), but no such migration exists anywhere in the codebase. A search for 'git-config migration', 'init-time git-config', and 'git config migration' returns zero hits in any source or doc file.
findings.location: AC2(c): '(contrast the old init-time git-config migration)'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the parenthetical contrast or replace with a concrete reference to the subprocess-spawning path it contrasts against (e.g., a named function in `_store/gitutil.py`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the test fixture's registry composition (e.g., 'a two-entry fixture registry with ids `["ensure-git-config", "ensure-hooks"]`') so the concurrency and idempotency ACs have a concrete expected value to assert against.
findings.finding: The 'registry ids' referenced in the concurrency AC (and implicitly in the idempotency and absent-marker ACs) are treated as a known vocabulary, but the plan never defines what the test fixture's registry contains, how many ids it holds, or how the test will construct the expected id set for comparison.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Concurrency test AC

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
